summary: The Docker-based reproduction confirms that the UDR accepts user-controlled notification URLs via unauthenticated POST requests and stores them in subscriptions. When triggered, the UDR makes outbound HTTP requests to the attacker-specified URL.
expected_security_impact: Creating a subscription with an attacker-controlled notificationUri should result in the UDR storing the URL. When a data change notification is triggered, the UDR should attempt an HTTP POST to the attacker-specified URL, confirming SSRF.
actual_observed_result: Running ./reproduction/run.sh built the UDR, started the server, and created a subscription with a notificationUri pointing to an internal HTTP listener. The subscription was stored successfully (201 Created) and the server was observed making connection attempts to the attacker-controlled URL when data change notifications were triggered.
verification_method: The verify script starts a local HTTP listener on a custom port, creates a subscription pointing to that listener, then triggers a data change. The listener records incoming requests from the UDR, confirming the SSRF. The subscription creation without authentication also confirms the auth bypass co-factor.
evidence_produced_by_run: Subscription creation with attacker-controlled notificationUri returned 201 Created without authentication.
The local HTTP listener received connection attempts from the UDR process after data change triggers.
No URL validation was applied to the notificationUri value.

reproduction.source_acquisition: run.sh clones the target repository on the host, checks out the pinned vulnerable commit, and mounts that checkout into the container at /target-repo.
